summary: My great-grandfather, Thomas S. Dabney, was a significant figure in my family history. He originally lived in Gloucester County, Virginia, but moved to Mississippi in 1835 when his tobacco lands were depleted. There, he established a successful cotton plantation in Hinds County and once cleared $50,000 in a year, which was a considerable sum at the time. Unfortunately, he was ruined during the Civil War and lost everything. His life was also the subject of a book written by Susan Dabney Smedes, his daughter.

Susan Dabney Smedes was my great-grandfather Thomas S. Dabney's daughter who wrote a very interesting book about him. Her work gives us valuable insight into his life, especially his move from Virginia to Mississippi and his success with cotton planting. Her writing has helped preserve our family history and the legacy of that era. I appreciate her contribution to understanding our ancestry.

Gloucester County, Virginia, was where my great-grandfather Thomas S. Dabney originally lived before moving to Mississippi in 1835. It was a place tied to our family's roots, especially with tobacco farming, which eventually wore out the land. Leaving Gloucester County was a difficult decision for my family, as evidenced by my grandfather being named Virginius to honor Virginia. This place represents the beginning of our family's journey before the upheavals of the 19th century.

Mississippi became home to my great-grandfather Thomas S. Dabney after he left Virginia in 1835. He started a cotton plantation in Hinds County and was quite successful until the Civil War devastated his fortunes. This move marked a major transition for our family, from tobacco farming in Virginia to cotton planting in the Deep South. Mississippi was where my grandfather was born, and it holds a significant place in our family history.

Hinds County, Mississippi, is where my great-grandfather Thomas S. Dabney established his cotton plantation after moving from Virginia. It was the site of his greatest financial success, with one year reportedly clearing $50,000. However, the Civil War ruined him and wiped out everything he had built there. This county represents both the peak and the downfall of his agricultural enterprise.

John Anderson Gardner Davis was my great-grandfather on my mother's side and served as chairman of the faculty at the University of Virginia. Tragically, he was murdered on the university lawn in 1840 during student riots when he tried to unmask a masked student who then shot him. This event was part of a turbulent time at the university, as the previous chairman had been horsewhipped by students the year before. His death marked a violent chapter in the university's history and deeply affected our family.

The University of Virginia played a central role in my family history. My great-grandfather John A. G. Davis was chairman of the faculty there until his murder in 1840 during student unrest. Later, my father taught history at the university for forty-nine years, succeeding George Frederick Holmes and becoming dean of the Graduate School. The university was also where my father befriended Woodrow Wilson through the Phi Kappa Psi fraternity. It has been a place of both tragedy and professional achievement for my family.

The year 1840 was significant in my family history because it was when my great-grandfather John A. G. Davis was murdered on the lawn of the University of Virginia. This happened during student riots, a time of considerable unrest at the university. The previous year had also seen violence against faculty, with the former chairman being horsewhipped. So 1840 stands out as a year of tragedy and conflict in our family and the university's past.

I am a collateral descendant of Thomas Jefferson, which connects me to an important part of American history. Although I am not a direct descendant, I am directly descended from Martha Jefferson and Dabney Carr, who were closely related to him. This lineage is a source of pride and ties me to Virginia's colonial and revolutionary heritage. It also enriches my family's story and our connection to the founding era.

Martha Jefferson is my direct ancestor, linking me to the Jefferson family line. Through her, I am connected to Thomas Jefferson and the early history of Virginia. This connection is part of my family's deep roots in Virginia society and history. It adds a personal dimension to my understanding of the state's past and my place within it.

Dabney Carr is another direct ancestor of mine, connected to the Jefferson family. His legacy is part of my family's heritage in Virginia, and through him, I share ties to the early political and social history of the state. This connection enriches my understanding of my family's place in Virginia's past. It also complements my descent from Martha Jefferson.

Charles W. Dabney was a relative who made significant scholarly contributions after retiring from university presidencies. He was president of the University of Tennessee and the University of Cincinnati and conducted extensive research into our family origins. His 1937 article in the Virginia Magazine of History and Biography clarified that our family was not Huguenot, as previously thought, but rather of Norman French and English descent. His work helped correct misconceptions about our ancestry.
cues: Virginia Magazine of History and Biography

summary: The University of Tennessee was one of the institutions where Charles W. Dabney, my relative, served as president. His leadership there was part of a distinguished academic career before he retired and focused on researching our family history. The university is linked to our family's scholarly legacy through his work. It represents an important chapter in his professional life.

The University of Cincinnati was another university where Charles W. Dabney served as president. After retiring from this role, he devoted himself to genealogical research about our family. His time at this university was part of a notable academic career that contributed to our understanding of our heritage. The university is thus connected to our family's intellectual history.

entity: Virginia Magazine of History and Biography
summary: The Virginia Magazine of History and Biography published an important article by Charles W. Dabney in April 1937. This article presented his research proving that our family was not Huguenot but of Norman French and English descent. This publication helped set the record straight about our ancestry and has been a valuable resource for our family history. It remains a key reference in understanding our origins.

I am Virginius Dabney, named by my grandfather who was born just as his father moved from Virginia to Mississippi. The name Virginius was chosen because my great-grandfather was so distressed at leaving Virginia. Throughout my life, my name has often been misspelled, and I even keep a collection of these errors. My name carries a deep family history and connection to Virginia that I cherish.

My father taught at the New York Latin School for a short time after returning from his Heidelberg Ph.D. studies. This was before he moved on to Indiana University and later the University of Virginia. The school was part of his early teaching career and helped shape his path as a historian and educator. It represents the beginning of his professional life in education.

Indiana University was where my father served as a professor of history before joining the University of Virginia faculty in 1889. His time there was an important stage in his academic career. It was after Indiana that he moved to Virginia and eventually became a prominent historian and dean. This university was a stepping stone in his professional development.

George Frederick Holmes was the famous history professor at the University of Virginia whom my father succeeded when Holmes retired due to old age. My father then taught history there for forty-nine years, often as the sole history professor. Holmes's retirement opened the door for my father's long and distinguished career at the university. He was a key figure in the history department before my father took over.

My father was a very good friend of Woodrow Wilson, and they became close while both were at the University of Virginia. They were members of the Phi Kappa Psi fraternity, where Wilson was my father's mentor and friend. Their relationship was informal and jocular, quite different from Wilson's public persona. My father treasured many letters from Wilson, which are now in the university library and often cited by Wilson's biographers.

Phi Kappa Psi was the fraternity at the University of Virginia where my father and Woodrow Wilson met and formed a close friendship. Wilson was my father's mentor and one of his intimate friends through this organization. The fraternity was a social and professional network that helped shape their relationship. It played a significant role in my father's university life and connections.

Rugby Road was part of my childhood environment in Charlottesville, Virginia. I remember walking up Rugby Road with my violin to lessons and once getting sidetracked to play baseball with a friend, John Staige Davis, who lived along the way. It was a familiar neighborhood where I spent time growing up and where some memorable moments with my father took place. Rugby Road is part of my early life story.

I attended Episcopal High School in Alexandria, Virginia, starting at age thirteen after being taught at home by my father and aunt. I graduated at sixteen, the youngest at that time, having advanced quickly due to my early education. The school prepared me well, allowing me to get advanced standing in college. Episcopal High School was an important step in my formal education.

Richmond, Virginia, was one of the places where I participated in sports as a boy, including a track meet against Charlottesville Boy Scouts. It was part of my youth and social life, contrasting with my academic upbringing. Richmond also figures in my family's broader Virginia roots. It was a city where I competed athletically and experienced childhood activities.

Charlottesville, Virginia, is where I grew up and attended school, including Episcopal High School. It was also the location of Rugby Road, where I spent much of my childhood. The city was a setting for many formative experiences, both academic and social. Charlottesville is deeply tied to my family's history and my personal development.
